items.foco: ICHD-3 criteria for migraine without aura
items.title: ICHD-3 criteria migraine without aura
items.contentHtml:
ICHD-3 criteria for migraine without aura

  • A: ≥5 attacks that meet B-D.
  • B: duration 4-72 h (without treatment or failed treatment).
  • C: ≥2 of: unilateral, pulsating, moderate-severe intensity, worsens with physical activity routine.
  • D: ≥1 of: nausea/vomiting, photo and phonophobia.
  • E: not better explained by another ICHD-3 diagnosis.
  • Examination trap: migraine without aura does not require aura for diagnosis; If there is aura, apply criteria for migraine with aura.

items.foco: Typical aura: visual, sensory or language symptoms
items.title: Typical aura symptoms
items.contentHtml:
Typical aura

  • Visual symptoms (most frequent): sparkling scotoma, zigzag lines, photopsias.
  • Sensory symptoms: unilateral paresthesias, typically slow progression (sensory gait).
  • Language symptoms: aphasia (dysnomia, paraphasias), without deficit motor.
  • Pathophysiology: propagated cortical depression (wave of neuronal depolarization followed by suppression of activity).
  • Trap: aura without headache occurs in migraine with typical aura; does not rule out the diagnosis.

items.foco: Migraine with aura: duration of aura 5-60 minutes
items.title: Duration of aura in migraine
items.contentHtml:
Duration of aura

  • ICHD-3 criteria: typical aura lasts between 5 and 60 minutes; if it is >60 min and <7 days, consider migraine with prolonged aura.
  • Criteria B of migraine with aura: ≥1 reversible aura symptom (visual, sensory, language).
  • Criteria C: ≥2 of: 1) at least one unilateral symptom, 2) gradual progression ≥5 min, 3) each symptom lasts 5-60 min.
  • Trap: if the aura lasts <5 min, suspect transient ischemic attack (TIA) or other secondary cause.

items.title: Acute migraine treatment
items.contentHtml:
Acute drugs

  • 1st line in mild-moderate migraine: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-800 mg, naproxen 500-1000 mg) or paracetamol 1000 mg.
  • Triptanes (sumatriptan 50-100 mg oral, 6 mg sc): 5-HT1B/1D agonists; effective in moderate-severe migraine.
  • Ergotamines (intranasal or IM dihydroergotamine): alternative, lower tolerance profile.
  • Golden rule: treat early in the attack; If a triptan fails, change to another with a different structure (naratriptan, rizatriptan).
  • Trap: ergotamines contraindicated in pregnancy and peripheral vascular disease.

items.title: Migraine prophylaxis
items.contentHtml:
Pharmacological prophylaxis

  • Indication: ≥4 attacks/month or ≥8 days of headache/month, or refractoriness to acute treatment.
  • Beta blockers: propranolol 40-240 mg/day (1st line).
  • Topiramate: 50-200 mg/day, start 25 mg and titrate; Adverse effect: paresthesias, weight loss.
  • Amitriptyline: 10-150 mg/day (especially if comorbid with insomnia or depression).
  • Trap: beta-blockers contraindicated in asthma and AV block; topiramate teratogenic (risk of cleft lip).

items.foco: Tension Headache vs Migraine: Key Features
items.title: DDx tension headache vs migraine
items.contentHtml:
Key differences

  • Tension headache: bilateral, oppressive (non-pulsatile), mild-moderate intensity, no nausea/vomiting, no significant photo/phonophobia.
  • Migraine: unilateral, pulsating, moderate-severe, worse with activity, associated with nausea and photo/phonophobia.
  • Duration: tension-type. 30 min-7 days; migraine 4-72 hours.
  • Trap: in chronic migraine there may be phenotypic overlap; use headache diary to differentiate.
  • Therapeutic test: response to triptans suggests migraine.

items.title: Status migrainosus and DHE
items.contentHtml:
State migrainous

  • Definition: migraine attack lasting >72 h, with pain-free intervals <4 h (without treatment).
  • Treatment of choice: dihydroergotamine (DHE) 0.5-1 mg IV/IM, repeat every 8 h (maximum 3 doses).
  • Alternatives: ketorolac 60 mg IM, metoclopramide 10 mg IV, corticosteroids (dexamethasone 8-16 mg IV).
  • Caution: DHE contraindicated if previous use of triptans in the last 24 hours (risk of vasospasm).
  • Trap: do not use oral ergotamine in state. migraine due to erratic absorption.

items.foco: Vestibular migraine: differential diagnosis with vertigo
items.title: Vestibular migraine and vertigo
items.contentHtml:
Vestibular migraine

  • Criteria: ≥5 episodes of vertigo of moderate-severe intensity, duration 5 min-72 h, associated with migraine (current or previous).
  • DDx with benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV): in BPPV the vertigo lasts seconds, triggered by postural changes, without headache.
  • DDx with Ménière's disease: fluctuating hearing loss, tinnitus, sensation of otic fullness.
  • Acute treatment: triptans (zolmitriptan 2.5 mg) or dimenhydrinate 50 mg.
  • Trap: vestibular migraine can occur without headache; Vertigo is the main symptom.

items.foco: Hemiplegic migraine: mutations in CACNA1A genes
items.title: Genetic hemiplegic migraine
items.contentHtml:
Hemiplegic migraine

  • Familial hemiplegic migraine (FHM): autosomal dominant; mutations in CACNA1A (P/Q type calcium channel, chromosome 19p13), ATP1A2 (Na+/K+ ATPase), SCN1A (sodium channel).
  • Clinical picture: motor aura (reversible hemiparesis) along with visual/sensory symptoms; aura duration up to 7 days.
  • Diagnosis: family history (≥1 1st or 2nd degree relative) and genetic study.
  • Treatment: avoid triptans and ergotamines (risk of vasospasm); use NSAIDs or calcium antagonists (verapamil) as prophylaxis.
  • Trap: in sporadic hemiplegic migraine, rule out TIA and vascular accident.

items.title: Migraine complications
items.contentHtml:
Complications

  • Chronic migraine: ≥15 days/month with headache for ≥3 months, of which ≥8 days meet the criteria for migraine.
  • Risk factors: excessive use of analgesics (≥10 days/month of triptans or ≥15 of NSAIDs).
  • Status migraine: attack lasting >72 h; treatment with DHE or corticosteroids.
  • Migraine infarction: ischemic aura with cerebral infarction documented on neuroimaging, in the context of a migraine attack with aura.
  • Trap: chronic migraine requires ruling out headache due to medication abuse (ICHD-3 criterion).

items.title: Contraindications of triptans
items.contentHtml:
Absolute contraindications

  • Cardiovascular disease: acute myocardial infarction, unstable angina, ischemic coronary disease, coronary vasospasm (Prinzmetal's angina).
  • Cerebrovascular disease: previous TIA or stroke.
  • Peripheral vascular disease (claudication intermittent).
  • Uncontrolled arterial hypertension (SBP >180 mmHg or DBP >110 mmHg).
  • Trap: triptans also contraindicated in hemiplegic migraine and migraine with brainstem aura due to theoretical risk of vasoconstriction.

items.foco: Indications for botulinum toxin in chronic migraine
items.title: Botulinum toxin in chronic migraine
items.contentHtml:
Botulinum toxin type A

  • Indication: chronic migraine (≥15 days/month, ≥8 with migraine) refractory to ≥2 oral prophylactics.
  • PREEMPT protocol: 155-195 U divided into 31-39 intramuscular points (muscles frontal, temporal, occipital, cervical).
  • Repeat every 12 weeks; evaluate response after 2 cycles.
  • Mechanism: inhibition of neurotransmitter release (CGRP, substance P) in nociceptive fibers.
  • Trap: do not use in episodic migraine (not approved); Adverse effect: ptosis, cervical weakness.
